coredata.dc:title: Gravity field-mediated synthesis of carbon-conjugated quantum dots with tunable defective density for enhanced triiodide reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Jiangwei
Song, Xuedan
Yu, Chang
Huang, Huawei
Hong, Jiafu
Ding, Yiwang
Huang, Hongling
Yu, Jinhe
Tan, Xinyi
Zhao, Zongbin
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Integration/alternation of the short- and long-range order carbon species into one is always a trade-off between exposed armchair or zig-zag sites for enhancing the density of local charge states and electronic conductivity for fastening charge transfer. However, the strategies available now for coupling suffer from multiple/complicated steps, which makes the carbon-conjugated microstructure and electronic structure of the carbon as well as the process difficult to be controlled. Herein, surface coupling engineering mediated by external gravity field enables the assembling of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with short-range periodicity on carbon sphere (CS) with long-range periodicity. The concentration gradient of CQDs on the surface of CS (CQDs/CS-x) can be finely controlled by the tuned gradient field strength, finally achieving the controllable regulation of defective density and electron transfer capability in carbon-conjugated CQDs. The resultant CQDs/CS-4 composite exhibits a high power conversion efficiency up to 8.42% with a low E pp value of 0.11 V, as probed by triiodide reduction. Taken together, density functional theory calculations and experimental results reveal that the edge/defective sites derived from CQDs modulate the charge density of local sites for enhanced capability of the adsorption for I2 molecule, and meanwhile the long-range π-π conjugated configuration of CS sites promotes the fast electron transfer in the carbon-conjugated CQDs. The design concept in this work presents a simple & facile technology for integration of the micro/nano-structured carbon materials with different scales to achieve the delicate regulation. Also, this provides fundamental guidance towards the assembly of ultrafine structure especially small-sized/ultrathin materials at atomic scales.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Edge carbon sites
Gravity field
Defective density
Triiodide reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CSi-sphere, C-sphere and Si-sphere

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51872035 , U1508201 ), and the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2016YFB0101201 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process and potential formation mechanism for CQDs/CS-x. (a) First step: in situ fabrication of CQDs in the system of CS. (b) Second step: scheme of the gravity field-mediated concentration distributions of CQDs, which are verified by the following UV–vis absorption spectra.

Chemical structure information of CSi-HT and CSi-sphere. (a) FT-IR spectra of CSi-HT and CSi-sphere. (b) 29Si MAS NMR spectrum of CSi-sphere. The inset in (a) highlights the slight red-shift of the C–O absorption peak, indicative of the weakened C–O–Si bond between silicates and carbon species.

The variation trend of defect densities and electron transfer ability of CQD/CS-x. (a) Illustration of the carbon-conjugated configuration. (b) Raman spectra and (c) linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of CQDs/CS-1, CQDs/CS-2, CQDs/CS-3, CQDs/CS-4 and CQDs/CS-5 to confirm the effects of the increased concentration of CQDs on its intrinsic performance from the view of the variation of defect densities and electron transfer ability.

SEM and TEM images of CSi-sphere and CQDs/CS-4, and its mass production. (a) and (b) SEM images of CSi-sphere. (c)–(e), the corresponding element mappings of C, Si and EDX spectrum, respectively. (f) and (g) SEM images of CQDs/CS-4. (h)–(j) the corresponding element mappings of C, Si and EDX spectrum, respectively. (k) photograph of mass production of CQDs/CS-4 sample, yielding ~2.4 g of CQDs/CS-4 product in a single preparation.

TEM images of CQDs/CS-4 composites and CQDs. (a) TEM image of CQDs/CS-4. (b) and (c) TEM images of the regions highlighted by red dashed box in (a). (d) a magnified HRTEM image of CQDs. (e) HRTEM image of single CQDs. Inset in (c): the corresponding size distribution of CQDs.

The electrocatalytic performance for a series of samples from CQDs/CS-1 to CQDs/CS-5 and the DFT calculation results. (a) The electrocatalytic performance for a series of samples from CQDs/CS-1 to CQDs/CS-5: the relationship between E pp and PCE. (b) The illustration of the coupled model for graphene sheets with long- and short-range order. (c)–(h) DFT calculation results: the adsorption energy of I2 molecule, the bonding length and the corresponding isosurface of charge density difference at different carbon sites including the basal plane, zig-zag, and armchair sites. (i) The relation and comparison between the gravity field strength (S), the microstructure and electrochemical performance of a series of CQDs/CS-x samples, such as the concentration of CQDs (C), defect density (D), electron transfer capability (T), and PCE (P). Every ring labelled by 1, 2, and 3 signifies low, medium and high levels, respectively. Also, the partial parameters mentioned in the figures are only quantitatively described.

head.ce:title: Gravity field-mediated synthesis of carbon-conjugated quantum dots with tunable defective density for enhanced triiodide reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, Liaoning, China

Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals
Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integration/alternation of the short- and long-range order carbon species into one is always a trade-off between exposed armchair or zig-zag sites for enhancing the density of local charge states and electronic conductivity for fastening charge transfer. However, the strategies available now for coupling suffer from multiple/complicated steps, which makes the carbon-conjugated microstructure and electronic structure of the carbon as well as the process difficult to be controlled. Herein, surface coupling engineering mediated by external gravity field enables the assembling of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with short-range periodicity on carbon sphere (CS) with long-range periodicity. The concentration gradient of CQDs on the surface of CS (CQDs/CS-x) can be finely controlled by the tuned gradient field strength, finally achieving the controllable regulation of defective density and electron transfer capability in carbon-conjugated CQDs. The resultant CQDs/CS-4 composite exhibits a high power conversion efficiency up to 8.42% with a low E pp value of 0.11 V, as probed by triiodide reduction. Taken together, density functional theory calculations and experimental results reveal that the edge/defective sites derived from CQDs modulate the charge density of local sites for enhanced capability of the adsorption for I2 molecule, and meanwhile the long-range π-π conjugated configuration of CS sites promotes the fast electron transfer in the carbon-conjugated CQDs. The design concept in this work presents a simple & facile technology for integration of the micro/nano-structured carbon materials with different scales to achieve the delicate regulation. Also, this provides fundamental guidance towards the assembly of ultrafine structure especially small-sized/ultrathin materials at atomic scales.

A simply processable and gravity field-mediated technique was presented to configure the carbon-conjugated quantum dots with tunable defective densities for achieving integration of long-/short-range order carbon species, and efficient and stable I3 − electroreduction.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials with the building unit of honeycomb graphene structure has shown remarkable features including excellent electric conductivity, mechanical property and high surface area [1]. Benefiting from these intriguing characteristics, they have been actively investigated in catalysis-related fields, and gradually utilized as highly promising candidates to replace noble metal catalysts [2–5]. Unfortunately, the sp2 hybridization of hexagonal carbon atoms in perfect graphene network endows itself with excellent electronic conductivity but inefficiently catalytic ability [6–8]. So the electronic structure of pristine graphene should be properly modulated to realize their potentially catalytic performance.

In this regard, various physical and chemical strategies have been made to modulate the electronic characteristics of carbon materials [9–17]. It has also been proved that the charge density of local states at the edges in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) would be considerably enhanced due to more armchair or zig-zag sites [18]. Furthermore, the armchair or zig-zag carbon sites with active unpaired π electron can facilitate electron transfer and contribute to a high activity for oxygen reduction reaction [4,14]. However, the electrocatalytic activity of these CQDs is seriously restricted by their poorly electroconductive ability and it is essential to integrate CQDs with other supports with long-range periodicity of conductive networks [14,16,19]. In spite of various routes for the synthesis of carbon-conjugated CQDs, the current existing issues, such as time-consuming and complex process, bring us a huge obstacle [20]. Hence, a novel and processable approach would be quite expected and it is meaningful for researchers to develop high-efficiency electrocatalysts that could integrate both the high catalytic activity of CQDs and the superior electronic conductivity ability of other graphene-based carbon materials.

As we know, the π-π conjugation between carbon and carbon species is the dominated interaction mode and the distribution density of CQDs on carbon substrates could be modulated to optimize the carbon-conjugated configuration. Concerning that the ultralight CQDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups could uniformly disperse in solutions due to the robust hydrogen-bonding with solvent molecules, so they are difficult to absolutely separate from the solutions. But the very fact may also provide new opportunities that if the local interactional balance could be destroyed by introducing external force (especially with controllable intensity). The sedimentation process of CQDs is capable of being tuned and finally results in the desired density distribution of CQDs in sedimentary products. Also, the post-synthetic strategy by ultracentrifugation had been demonstrated to be highly effective for the sorting of carbon materials with subtle difference in diameters, e.g. single-, double- and multi-walled carbon nanotubes [21–24].

Inspired by this, we present a simply processable and gravity field-mediated technique to configure the carbon sphere (CS)-conjugated CQDs with tunable defective density (CQDs/CS-x). The controllable modulation of CQDs concentration distribution is highly compatible with the strength of gravity field. Benefiting from this, it is expected that this technique will directly satisfy the requirements for the CQDs with desired concentration in various large-scale applications. Generally, the CQDs can be firstly in situ synthesized during the formation process of CS, which would be seldomly concerned in some case due to the super small molecule structure of CQDs. The delicate regulation of defective density in carbon-conjugated CQDs could be easily realized by the assistance of gravity field with different intensities. In addition, a series of carbon-conjugated CQDs with varied defective densities were engineered and thoroughly exploited for triiodide reduction (IRR). The CQDs/CS-4 catalyst exhibits a superior performance, which achieves a superior power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to 8.42% and a low E pp value of 0.11 V. The excellent performance is attributed to the low adsorption energy of I2 molecule occurring at the edge sites of carbon (especially armchair sites), and the synergetic effects and an optimal balance between edge sites and electron transfer capability were proved to be essential for IRR. This work highlights the new protocol for in situ fabrication of CQDs, and the effects of mechanical force in regulation of defective densities for microstructure of carbon species. Also, we emphasize the importance of edge carbon sites in tailoring the electronic property and thus creating the desired active sites, which may be helpful for rational electrocatalyst design.

The synthesis of CQDs/CS-x samples mainly contains two sequent procedures, as shown in Fig. 1 . In the first step (Fig. 1a), the CQDs derived from the confinement of silicate species were in situ formed during the preparation process of CS. The concentration of CQDs in CQDs/CS-x could be further modulated with the assistance of gravity field in the following step (Fig. 1b). Specifically, the uniform solution of resol precursors was firstly prepared, and then tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) as silica source was added into the above solution. When subjected to hydrothermal treatment, the TEOS dissociated and assembled together with resol precursors to form phenolic resin polymer-silicate composite (CSi-HT). This process was followed by the high temperature carbonization, etching, gravity field-mediated modulation, finally producing the CQDs/CS-x samples. It was found that the gravity field with controllable strength was applied to produce a series of CQDs/CS-x with controllable concentration distribution of CQDs on the CS support, indicative of a gravity-sensitive and dependent behavior. This is also further discussed and confirmed in the following section.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of CSi-HT precursor (Fig. 2 a) confirms the framework of silicates-resin polymer. The peaks at ~465 and 1100 cm−1 arising from Si–O–Si bonds have been detected, revealing that TEOS are completely converted to silicates. This is coincident with the results of 29Si solid-state magic-angle-spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (29Si MAS NMR) (Fig. 2b), where the tetrahedral Si sites of OSi–O (−110 ppm) is detected. Besides, no signal is detected at −60 ppm, indicating that no direct Si–C bonds at the interface are formed. The peak at ~1630 cm−1 arising from the aromatic structures confirms the network of phenolic resins [25]. After high temperature carbonization, the FTIR peaks of CSi-sphere at 1375, 1465 and 2750-3000 cm−1 belonging to the C–N and C–H stretching of CTAB molecules almost disappear in comparison with that of CSi-HT (Fig. 2a), indicating the decomposition and removal of CTAB, which is also verified by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermogravimetry (DTG) results (Fig. S1). Fig. S2 depicts the typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of CSi-sphere. The adsorption peak at ca. 265 nm indicates the presence of π–π* electron transition occurring on the surface of CQDs [26]. The fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs are further measured at different excitation wavelengths and the corresponding excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CQDs (Fig. S3) is clearly observed, which well agrees with other reports [27]. For comparison, the carbon sphere (C-sphere) and silicon sphere (Si-sphere) were also prepared following the same procedures without Si and C source, respectively (Fig. S4). However, not any peaks are observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S2). Therefore, it can be envisaged that the silicate species play a crucial role for the in situ fabrication of CQDs. The silicates should act as confined nanoreactors and some parts of resin polymers are wrapped by Si–O–C bonds (Figs. 1a and 2). As a result, only the resin polymers in the silicates-confined reactors can thermally polymerize with each other to in situ form CQDs.

It is interesting to note that the peak intensities for UV–vis absorption spectra at ca. 265 nm gradually increase with an enhancement of gravity-field strength and follow the order: CQDs/CS-1 < CQDs/CS-2 < CQDs/CS-3 < CQDs/CS-4 < CQDs/CS-5 (Fig. 1b). This is also mainly attributed to the increased concentration/density of CQDs on the surface of CS [28]. To further investigate the optical properties of the obtained CQDs, more detailed PL studies at different excitation wavelengths were conducted. As shown in excitation-dependent PL spectra (Figs. S5a–f), the corresponding excitation-dependent PL behaviors are obviously observed. The intensity of the emission peaks gradually increase following the increment of excitation wavelengths and the strongest peak appears at 280 nm, and then the peak positions shift to longer wavelengths with reduced intensity, which are similar to that of CQDs reported in other work [29,30]. In addition, following the increased proportion of CQDs with short-range order in the carbon-conjugated configuration, more edge sites are exposed and correspondingly the electronic structure will be altered (Fig. 3 a). Expectedly, Raman spectra (Fig. 3b) evidence that the defect density increase from 0.93 (CQDs/CS-1) to 1.00 (CQDs/CS-2), 1.10 (CQDs/CS-3), 1.16 (CQDs/CS-4) and finally up to 1.37 (CQDs/CS-5). Also, the slopes of LSV curves (Fig. 3c) gradually decrease from CQDs/CS-1 to CQDs/CS-5, revealing the gradually increased electrical resistance, which is a common sense in graphene-based functional carbon materials. In summary, controllable regulating the defect density and electron transfer ability of the samples can be realized with the assistance of gravity field.

Furthermore, for simplification, the sample CQDs/CS-4 was taken as an example to investigate the structural evolution during this process. As observed in the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 4 a, b, f, g), the CSi-sphere after carbonization and acid-treated CQDs/CS-4 show a relatively rough surface compared with CSi-HT after hydrothermal crystallization (Figs. S6a and b). Also, the Si percentage of CSi-sphere significantly decreases from 4.02 to 0.11 at % for CQDs/CS-4 (Fig. 4c–e and h-j), indicating that the silicates are almost removed, as further confirmed by TGA and FTIR analysis (Figs. S7 and 8a). The specific surface area of CSi-HT also increases from 44 to 980 m2/g for CSi-sphere, and further to 1498 m2/g for CQDs/CS-4 (Figs. S9–S10 and Table S1). Additionally, the CQDs/CS-4 can be easily scaled up with an excellent yield, being capable of meeting the requirements of large-scale production (Fig. 4k).

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 5 a–c) further display that a large amount of CQDs with an average diameter of 4.0 nm can be observed in the vision scope. Well-resolved lattice with a spacing of 0.31 nm is clearly observed from the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images (Fig. 5d and e), corresponding to (002) facet of graphite. The crystal structure, elemental components and carbon bonding configurations were further analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. As shown in XRD pattern (Fig. S8b), a strong diffraction peak at 23.4° attributed to π-π stacking between two overlapped layers is observed, which is consistent to the TEM results. Referring to XPS survey spectra (Fig. S8c), the as-made CQDs/CS-4 sample mainly consists of C and O elements. Based on the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. S8d), four peaks centered at 284.6, 286.3, 288.9 and 290.8 eV corresponding to C–C, C–O, CO and π–π* bonds are observed. These surface oxygen-containing species can be confirmed by the results of FTIR (Fig. S8a).

It is commonly known that the low-cost and alternative Pt CEs for IRR is very essential for practical application of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [31,32]. As descripted above, the designed CQDs/CS-x feature the unique characteristics such as long-range periodicity of graphene networks that can well serve as a conductive substrate, and the abundant and tunable activity sites/defect densities derived from the CQDs can well catalyze the electroreduction of triiodide. Benefiting from these, the CQDs/CS-x could be one of ideal materials for Pt replacement of DSSCs. As such, the electrocatalytic performance and the photovoltaic performance (J-V) of a series of CQDs/CS-x were further evaluated, and the detailed experimental results are schemed in Fig. 6 a, Figs. S11–S17, and Table S2.

In detail, all the CQDs/CS-x samples possessed high values of PCE compared with that of Pt (7.35%). Especially, for CQDs/CS-4, the PCE of 8.42% has been be achieved. To further explore the potential mechanism for the improved performance, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was implemented to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of samples towards the reduction of I3 −. The typical pair of A red/A ox peaks that are associated with the reduction and oxidation reactions: I3 − + 2e− → 3I− and 3I− ̶ 2e− → I3 −, respectively, are clearly observed for all the CEs (Fig. S18). Generally, the A ox and A red peaks and a small potential separation (E pp) between these two peaks generally can help to clearly clarify an excellently electrocatalytic performance. Obviously, the current density of A red peaks for all CEs has a little change, while the E pp values vary notably and follow the order: Pt (0.28 V) > CQDs/CS-1 (0.20 V) > CQDs/CS-2 (0.19 V) > CQDs/CS-3 (0.16 V) > CQDs/CS-5 (0.14 V) > CQDs/CS-4 (0.11 V). As such, the CQDs/CS-4 has the highest catalytic activity. Moreover, it is well known that a high exchange current density (J 0) also corresponds to a high electrocatalytic activity. As shown in Tafel polarization curves of identical dummy cells made of Pt or CQDs/CS-x CEs (Fig. S19), the values of J 0 follow the order: CQDs/CS-4 > CQDs/CS-5 > CQDs/CS-3 > CQDs/CS-2 > CQDs/CS-1 > Pt, corresponding to a gradual decrease in catalytic capability.

To further explore the potential reason for the improved performance, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements (EIS) were conducted to study charge transfer process of the identical dummy cells made of Pt or CQDs/CS-4 CEs and the detailed parameters were obtained with an EIS spectra analyzer (Fig. S20, Table S3). The series resistance (R s) of CQDs/CS-4 (4.0 Ω cm2) is smaller than that of Pt (9.1 Ω cm2), indicating that CQDs/CS-4 owns better affinity towards the FTO glasses (Fig. S20a). In addition, the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of CQDs/CS-4 (0.1 Ω cm2) is much smaller than that of Pt (0.5 Ω cm2), which is consistent to the results of J 0 (Fig. S19) and reflects the better catalytic activity of CQDs/CS-4. The electrochemical stability of the CE materials that is related to the large-scale application of DSSCs, was also tested for Pt and CQDs/CS-4 CEs (Figs. S20b and c). It is clearly shown that the R ct of Pt CE increases noticeably, while that of CQDs/CS-4 CE hardly changes, implying the superior electrochemical stability of CQDs/CS-4 CE. Furthermore, the reduction reaction kinetics of I3 − for Pt and CQDs/CS-4 samples were investigated and the corresponding diffusion coefficient (D) was calculated by Randles–Sevcik theory (the detailed calculation process was shown in Fig. S21). The value of D (3.28 × 10−4 cm−2 s−1) for CQDs/CS-4 CE is larger than that for Pt (0.88 × 10−4 cm−2 s−1) (Table S4), thus suggesting fast IRR electrocatalytic kinetics, which is consistent with the limited current density (J lim, Fig. S19).

As described above, the CQDs on the surface of CS significantly improve the electrocatalytic reduction capability of CS towards I3 −. To well present the enhanced reaction behaviors, DFT calculations were adopted and carried out to uncover the intrinsic contribution to activity. The considered adsorption sites on carbon surface towards I2 molecule mainly include the basal plane, zig-zag, and armchair carbon sites. As shown in Fig. 6c–e, the adsorption energy of I2 molecule on the basal plane is only - 0.03 eV, while the adsorption energy of I2 molecule at the zig-zag carbon sites is reduced to - 0.21 eV, and the adsorption energy at the armchair carbon sites further decreases to - 0.65 eV. Correspondingly, the bonding length between I2 molecule and adsorption sites also decreases from 10.99 Å for basic plane to 3.75 Å for zig-zag carbon sites, and further to 3.08 Å for armchair carbon sites. These results further indicate that the armchair carbon with charge-richer local states better benefits for the adsorption of I2 molecule [33]. This is also the case for the isosurface of charge density difference (Fig. 6f–h), demonstrating that the I2 molecules can be preferably adsorbed at the armchair carbon sites to achieve the whole reduction.

On the basis of this, the experimental results could be well explained and decoupled, where the CQDs on the CS surface feature more edge carbon sites (defect sites) with enhanced charge density and can further serve as active sites to be beneficial for the adsorption of I2 molecule, as schemed in Fig. 6b. Moreover, it should be noted that an optimization and balance between the density of defective sites and electron transfer capability for the carbon-conjugated configuration is also significant in view of the electrocatalytic activity. To clearly demonstrate this point, the structure-performance relationship between the gravity field strength, the microstructure and electrochemical performance of the samples were also summarized in Fig. 6i. It can be obviously observed that for samples from CQDs/CS-1 to CQDs/CS-4 with the gradually increased distribution density of CQDs on the surface of CS, the PCE gradually increases and reaches to the maximum primarily due to more exposed defective/active sites. While, the continuously increased defective sites no longer lead to an increase of PCE for CQDs/CS-5, although much more active sites are exposed in comparison to that of CQDs/CS-4. Therefore, the electron transfer capability may exert more critical roles here and an optimal balance between them is essential for highly effective carbon-based IRR electrocatalyst.

In summary, we have proposed a novel gravity field-assisted material assembly strategy to directly achieve the integration and coupling of CS with long-range periodic carbon structure and CQDs with short-range order. With the assistance of gravity field, different concentration gradient of CQDs can be easily assembled on the surface of CS, producing the carbon-conjugated configuration with the delicate regulation of defective densities (edge sites). Significantly, the controllable modulation of defective density provides a perfect model system to probe and quantitatively establish the correlation between the intrinsic actives sites of all-carbon material and its catalytic performance, which, for example, successfully identifies CQDs/CS-4 to be the best active IRR catalyst. The value of PCE reaches up to 8.42% with a lowest E pp (0.11 V). Experimental studies combined with theoretical calculations have demonstrated that I2 molecule would prefer to be adsorbed at edge sites, especially at armchair carbon sites. The gravity field-assisted technology presents the ultra superiorities including simpleness, quickness, easy processibility in configuring the carbon-carbon homogeneous architechtures. Also, it will provide efficient and potential possibilities for constructing other homostructure or heterostructure advanced materials with different mass density differences, thus broadening the range for practical application.

Ammonia aqueous solution (25 wt%) and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. CTAB (25 wt% in water), formalin solution (37 wt%) and TEOS were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Company. All chemicals were directly used without any further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CSi-sphere, C-sphere and Si-sphere
ce:section.ce:para: The resol precursor solution was prepared according to previous work [34]. In a typical procedure, 0.4 ml of ammonia solution was mixed with 4 ml of ethanol solution, 38 ml deionized water, 0.60 ml formalin solution 2.08 g of CTAB and 0.40 g resorcinol, then continually stirred to form homogeneous solution. After 3 h, 1.44 ml TEOS was added dropwise into the above solution and continually stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. Then, the mixtures were transferred into 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermal crystallization was carried out at 100 °C for 24 h under static conditions. The as-synthesized slurry was then centrifuged, washed with ethanol for 6 times, and dried at 80 °C for 10 h to obtain solid products (named as CSi-HT). Calcination was carried out in a horizontally placed tubular quartz tube at 600 °C for 2 h in a flowing N2, and then we increased the temperature to 800 °C with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and maintained it for 5 h to yield carbon-silica composites, named as CSi-sphere. For comparison, the C-sphere and Si-sphere were synthesized following the similar way without the TEOS and formalin solution and resorcinol, respectively.

The as-prepared CSi-sphere was firstly subjected to desilication. Generally, 1.0 g of CSi-sphere was slurried in 10 ml of dilute HF solution (10 wt%) and stirred at 30 °C for 12 h to remove silicates. The nearly complete removal of silica species from the C–Si spheres was confirmed by TGA. Then the solution was dialyzed (3500 Da) for 2 days to reach neutral. For the synthesis of CQD/CS-x, the above solution was dispersed into 50 mL ethanol solution and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the suspension was centrifuged at 6000, 8000, 10000, 12000 and 14000 rpm and the precipitates were further dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain CQD/CS-1, CQD/CS-2, CQD/CS-3, CQD/CS-4 and CQD/CS-5, respectively. The corresponding mass ratio of CQDs in these carbon-conjugated quantum dots samples gradually increases, as confirmed from UV–vis absorption spectra.

The samples prepared above were firstly grinded uniformly with carboxyethyl cellulose solution. The as-prepared slurries were deposited directly onto fluorine-doped tin oxide glasses (FTO) by a doctor-blade method. After that, the glass was treated at 500 °C in N2 to obtain various CEs.

Assembly of DSSCs: The DSSCs was assembled following our previous work [32]. Briefly, TiO2 photoanodes were firstly dipped into an anhydrous ethanol solution of N719 dye (5 × 10−4 M) for ~20 h to absorb the dye molecules, followed by washing with absolute ethanol and drying. Then, the TiO2 photoanodes and obtained CEs separated by a hot-melt surlyn film were assembled by the hot-press technology. The I3 −/I− electrolyte was injected through the predrilled hole on the FTO glass into the space between two electrodes and the hole was then sealed by scotch tape.
Assembly of identical dummy cells: The dummy cells, in which the photoanodes of the real cell were replaced by the CEs consisting of two identical CEs.

XRD were conducted with Smart Lab 9 kW equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The HRTEM images of the samples were taken by FEI Tecnai F30. The morphology of the materials were observed by SEM (HITACHI SU8220). The information of chemical composition and electronic structure were characterized by XPS (Thermo ESCALAB 250), Raman (HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR), FTIR (Bruker EQUINOX 55) and 29Si MAS NMR (Agilent DD 2500 MHz). TGA (NETZSCH STA 449 F3) was carried out on a thermal analyzer in N2 at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of samples were performed on a Micromeritics 3Flex 3500 instrument. The UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermofisher, EVOLUTION 220) and the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000), respectively.

The J-V curves were measured under AM 1.5G and 1000 W m−2 by an AAA solar simulator (94032A, Newport, US) equipped with a Keithley 2400 source meter. The electrocatalytic active area of cells subjected to photovoltaic testing was 0.16 cm2. CV was implemented at various scan rates in a three-electrode system, where Pt wire was employed as the counter electrode, the as-prepared CEs as working electrode and Ag/Ag+ electrode as reference electrode in an acetonitrile solution containing 1 mM I2, 10 mM LiI and 100 mM LiClO4. For the measurements of Tafel polarization and EIS, the fabricated dummy cells were applied and the Tafel plots, EIS spectra and linear sweep voltammetry curves were all characterized by multichannel potentiostats (VSP, Bio-Logic, France).

The calculation reported in this work was carried out within the DFT using the Vienna ab initio simulation package [35]. Generalized gradient approximation with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof exchange-correlation functional was adopted to handle the exchange-correlation interaction [36]. The energy convergence was selected 1 × 10−4 eV atom−1 and a cutoff for the plane wave of 520 eV was employed. Graphene with single atomic layer was modeled with vacuum widths of 15 Å.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51872035, U1508201), and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0101201).

ce:section.ce:para:
